data.narrative:
Dawn Aerospace, a Series B space and aerospace company that has raised $30M+, is evaluated against a peer universe of 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers, with 3 same-stage private comparables applied directly to valuation.

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-Progression applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier to the $30M total raised proxy, implying $57M. Peer-Median anchors to three Series B space and aerospace comparables—Aalyria ($1.3B), Hubble Network ($400M), and Sift ($274M)—yielding a median of $400M and a range of $274M to $1.3B. Public-Comp Anchoring cannot be applied; while the public space and aerospace sector shows a 59.8x revenue multiple, Dawn Aerospace's revenue is not disclosed.

The two applicable methods produce a wide spread. Triangulating Stage-Progression ($57M) and Peer-Median ($400M) yields a central range of $48M to $460M, with a midpoint of $228M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty in how early-stage private aerospace valuations map to the demonstrated range of late-stage peers. Stage-Progression suggests a conservative floor anchored to capital deployed; Peer-Median captures actual recent market transactions but includes outliers (Aalyria) that may not be directly comparable. Without revenue visibility or clarity on technology differentiation relative to peers, the range widens considerably. A valuation in the $100M–$250M band appears most defensible relative to stage and raised capital, though the upper quartile cannot be ruled out if technology or market position warrants premium positioning within the peer set.
